cases.question: As of January 1, 2024, will SpaceX conduct at least 100 orbital launches in calendar year 2024?

output.method: Correlated component aggregation: used the component mean and median as anchors, treated the three forecasts as highly correlated because they rely on the same fixed evidence packet and similar reference classes, then made no material adjustment because there were no resolution-boundary errors and the calibration warnings were already reflected in the mid-70s probabilities.
output.rationale: The component forecasts are tightly clustered at 72%, 74%, and 76%, giving both a mean and median of 74%. The core evidence supports a probability well above 50%: SpaceX had already completed 96 orbital launches in 2023, so the 100-launch threshold required only about 4.2% growth, with late-2023 cadence near the needed run rate and Starlink providing recurring internal demand. The main counterweight is execution risk: a Falcon 9 grounding, range or pad constraints, weather, or customer delays could erase a small four-launch margin. Because all components use the same evidence and similar reasoning, I do not sharpen for apparent agreement. The evidence is also narrow and operational-risk-heavy, so I avoid moving above the component consensus. Final aggregate: 74%.
output.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","url":"provided-in-prompt","claim":"SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023 after 61 in 2022 and 31 in 2021; late-2023 cadence was near twice weekly; risks included grounding, range constraints, weather, pad limits, customer delays, and Starship attention."}]
output.calibration_notes: Agreement is high, but it is not independent agreement. The best-supported base rate in the components is 65%, while the inside-view arguments consistently justify an upward adjustment into the low-to-mid 70s. The aggregate keeps the median rather than pushing higher because the same correlated blind spots appear across all components.
output.calibration_warnings: ["Component forecasts are role-differentiated but highly correlated because they use the same fixed packet and similar reference classes.","The reference class is narrow and based mostly on SpaceX's recent trajectory rather than a broad outside-view distribution.","The outcome is threshold-sensitive: a small number of delayed launches can decide resolution.","Execution risks are correlated; one Falcon 9, range, pad, or weather disruption could affect multiple launches.","No external or post-cutoff information is used."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. I use a narrow reference class of SpaceX year-ahead launch cadence continuation after a large recent increase. In the fixed evidence, SpaceX grew from 31 launches in 2021 to 61 in 2022 to 96 in 2023, so the recent base pattern is strong year-over-year growth, but based on only two transitions and therefore fragile. A 100-launch outcome requires only about 4% growth over 2023, much less than the prior increases, and late-2023 cadence was already near twice weekly. I adjust upward from the base rate because the threshold is close to the already achieved 2023 level and Starlink creates internal launch demand. I do not push above the mid-70s because sustaining record cadence for a full year leaves meaningful exposure to Falcon 9 grounding, range/weather/pad constraints, customer delays, and operational diversion toward Starship.
attempts.strongest_yes: The target is barely above the already completed 2023 total: after 96 launches in 2023 and near twice-weekly cadence by late 2023, reaching 100 in 2024 requires continuity more than another major step-change.
attempts.strongest_no: At this cadence, even a modest Falcon 9 grounding, range bottleneck, or weather/pad disruption could erase the four-launch margin needed to reach 100.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether late-2023 near twice-weekly cadence was sustainable across all of 2024.","Probability and duration of any Falcon 9 grounding or major anomaly.","How much range, weather, and pad capacity constrain realized launch count.","Whether Starlink demand and payload readiness remain sufficient all year.","Whether Starship work meaningfully diverts operational focus from Falcon 9 cadence."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the most likely story is not that 100 was structurally impossible, but that a grounding, range/weather accumulation, pad bottleneck, or payload readiness delays caused several weeks of lost cadence, leaving SpaceX just below the threshold despite strong underlying demand.
attempts.wildcards: ["A major Falcon 9 anomaly causing a long grounding.","Unexpected range or pad availability expansion improving cadence.","Starlink payload production or deployment priorities changing materially.","A severe weather season affecting multiple launch windows.","Starship test activity either consuming resources or having little operational effect on Falcon cadence."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023 after 61 in 2022 and 31 in 2021; most launches were Falcon 9 Starlink-related missions; late-2023 cadence was near twice weekly; main risks included Falcon 9 grounding, range constraints, weather, pad limits, customer delays, and Starship transition attention."}]
attempts.reference_class: SpaceX annual orbital launch totals in the immediately preceding high-growth cadence period, asking whether the next calendar year clears a modest threshold just above the prior record year.
attempts.resolution_boundary: Resolve true if SpaceX completed 100 or more orbital launches between 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC and 2024-12-31 23:59 UTC; otherwise resolve false.
attempts.evidence_for: ["SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023, so the threshold requires only 4 additional launches, about 4.2% growth.","The prior sequence was 31 launches in 2021, 61 in 2022, and 96 in 2023, indicating strong recent cadence growth.","Most launches supported Starlink, suggesting recurring internal demand rather than dependence only on external customers.","Operational cadence was already near twice weekly by late 2023, which annualizes to roughly the required level.","The company had publicly discussed very high launch cadence ambitions."]
attempts.evidence_against: ["The company would need to sustain record cadence for another full year, not merely briefly touch it.","Falcon 9 grounding risk could remove enough launch opportunities to miss by a small margin.","Range constraints, weather, and pad limits could create cumulative slippage.","Customer delays could reduce non-Starlink launches.","Management and engineering attention toward Starship could distract from Falcon cadence, even if Falcon 9 remained the main workhorse."]
attempts.calibration_warnings: ["The reference class is narrow and based heavily on only the 2021-2023 SpaceX trajectory, so the base rate is weak.","The evidence packet gives no broader historical frequency for organizations sustaining record orbital cadence after rapid growth.","Several assumptions are correlated: Starlink demand, Falcon 9 reliability, pad throughput, and launch cadence all depend on the same operational system.","The small gap from 96 to 100 supports a high probability, but a small miss is also plausible because only a few delayed launches decide the outcome.","Avoiding external information means no use of actual 2024 manifests, anomaly reports, or launch schedules beyond the fixed packet."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. The threshold is very close to the known 2023 result: 100 requires only 4 more launches than 96, or about 4.2% growth. My reference class is a high-cadence launch provider coming off a record year with demonstrated multi-year growth and an internally driven recurring payload stream, asked to exceed the prior-year total by a low single-digit percentage. I start around 65% because marginal year-over-year growth after a record operational year is more likely than not, but not automatic due to reliability, range, pad, and weather constraints. Case-specific evidence moves this up: 31 to 61 to 96 shows strong realized scaling momentum; late-2023 cadence was already near twice weekly, roughly enough for the target if sustained; Starlink demand reduces dependence on external customer timing; and the target is not a large stretch. I subtract meaningful probability for a Falcon 9 grounding or repeated launch-flow disruption, because a few lost weeks could erase the narrow margin, and for the challenge of sustaining record cadence across a full calendar year. Net: the inside view lands in the mid-to-high 70s rather than above 85 because the main failure modes are correlated and operationally material.
attempts.strongest_yes: The target is only four launches above a demonstrated 96-launch year, and late-2023 cadence was already around the level needed for 100 if maintained, with Starlink providing a repeatable internal mission pipeline.
attempts.strongest_no: The margin above 2023 is numerically small but operationally fragile: a grounding, pad/range bottleneck, or several weather-driven slips could quickly consume the four-launch cushion and prevent another record year.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Probability and duration of any Falcon 9 grounding in 2024.","Whether late-2023 twice-weekly cadence can be sustained across a full calendar year.","How binding range, pad, recovery, and weather constraints become at roughly 100 launches per year.","How much of the 2024 manifest is Starlink-like internally controlled demand versus externally delayed customer missions.","Whether management attention toward Starship materially competes with Falcon 9 launch operations."]
attempts.premortem: If this resolves false, the most likely story is not that 100 was intrinsically out of reach, but that one or two operational interruptions consumed the small margin: a Falcon 9 issue causes a multi-week standdown, range or pad availability compresses the schedule, weather causes cascading slips, and the company finishes in the 90s despite strong baseline cadence.
attempts.wildcards: ["A major Falcon 9 anomaly or regulatory standdown lasting several weeks or months.","Unexpectedly smooth operations allowing cadence above twice weekly for extended periods.","Severe weather or range congestion concentrated around already tight launch windows.","A strategic shift that prioritizes Starship development at the expense of Falcon 9 cadence."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023 after 61 in 2022 and 31 in 2021, with most launches supporting Starlink and late-2023 cadence near twice weekly."}]
attempts.reference_class: High-cadence orbital launch operators or launch systems that have just set a record year and need only low single-digit percentage growth in the following year, especially where much of the manifest is internally generated rather than customer-scheduled.
attempts.resolution_boundary: Resolves true if SpaceX completed 100 or more orbital launches between 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC and 2024-12-31 23:59 UTC; otherwise false. Only completed orbital launches count.
attempts.evidence_for: ["SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023, so the 100-launch threshold requires only 4 additional launches, about 4.2% growth.","The trend was strongly upward: 31 launches in 2021, 61 in 2022, and 96 in 2023.","Most launches were Falcon 9 missions supporting Starlink, implying a large internal demand driver and less reliance on external customer readiness.","Operational cadence was already near twice weekly by late 2023, which annualizes to roughly the required level.","The company had publicly discussed very high launch cadence ambitions, suggesting organizational focus and incentive alignment toward exceeding 100."]
attempts.evidence_against: ["The 2023 result was already a record cadence, so sustaining or modestly exceeding it for another full year is operationally demanding.","A Falcon 9 grounding could remove enough launch opportunities to miss the threshold.","Range constraints, weather, and pad limits could create cumulative delays even without a major failure.","Customer delays can still affect non-Starlink missions and create manifest inefficiency.","Attention or resources shifting toward Starship could modestly distract from Falcon 9 operational execution."]
attempts.calibration_warnings: ["Base-rate evidence is weak because the reference class is narrow and SpaceX's cadence is unusual.","The evidence packet gives only aggregate annual counts and qualitative constraints, not a month-by-month manifest or pad capacity model.","Several risks are correlated: a launch anomaly can trigger grounding, schedule compression, customer delays, and range conflicts together.","Inside-view confidence may be too high because recent rapid growth can create trend-extrapolation bias.","No external information or post-2024-01-01 evidence is used by instruction."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. The fixed evidence says SpaceX reached 96 orbital launches in 2023 after rapid growth from 31 in 2021 and 61 in 2022, with cadence already near twice weekly by late 2023. A threshold of 100 requires only 4 more launches than 2023, so the base case is above even odds. I start from a reference class of an organization that has just set a launch-cadence record and needs low-single-digit growth the following year; that suggests roughly 65%, because sustaining a record operational tempo is easier than another doubling but still exposed to disruption. The inside view is stronger: Starlink provides repeatable internal demand, cadence was already close to the required pace, and public ambitions imply capacity planning above 100. I do not push much higher because launch systems are brittle to groundings, weather, pad/range constraints, and customer delays, and these risks are correlated: one Falcon 9 or range issue can remove multiple launches from the schedule. Final probability: 72%.
attempts.strongest_yes: The target is only marginally above the already achieved 2023 count of 96, and late-2023 cadence was already near the required run rate, with Starlink providing a repeatable demand engine.
attempts.strongest_no: The forecast depends on uninterrupted execution at record cadence; a single meaningful Falcon 9 grounding, pad/range bottleneck, or cluster of weather delays could easily cost more than the 4-launch margin over 2023.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Falcon 9 avoids any multi-week grounding or systemic technical issue during 2024.","Whether late-2023 cadence was a sustainable operating level or partly a temporary surge.","Whether range, pad, and recovery operations can support the required annual throughput without accumulating delays.","How much of the 2024 manifest is flexible Starlink demand versus externally constrained customer missions.","Whether Starship-related attention materially distracts from Falcon launch operations."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong on the high side, the most likely story is that the 96-launch 2023 result made 100 look like a small step, but the final launches were squeezed out by a brief grounding, range congestion, bad weather windows, or pad/recovery constraints. Because the required margin is only 4 launches, even one month of reduced cadence could be decisive. Another failure mode is assuming Starlink demand converts directly into completed orbital launches; actual launch completion also depends on vehicle availability, range slots, recovery assets, and payload readiness. Resolution ambiguity is limited but still relevant: only completed orbital launches count, so failed attempts, suborbital Starship flights, or launches slipping across the UTC year boundary would not help. If this forecast is wrong on the low side, it is because the 2023 trend and late-year cadence were more durable than a cautious operational-risk model allowed, and SpaceX simply continued Falcon 9 reuse and Starlink deployment at or above the existing pace.
attempts.wildcards: ["A major Falcon 9 anomaly or regulatory grounding.","Unusually persistent launch-site weather disruption.","Range or pad infrastructure bottlenecks becoming binding.","Unexpected acceleration from operational learning and fleet reuse.","Manifest reshuffling that replaces delayed customer missions with Starlink missions quickly enough to preserve cadence."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet: SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023 after 61 in 2022 and 31 in 2021; most were Falcon 9 missions supporting Starlink; late-2023 cadence was near twice weekly; main risks included grounding, range constraints, weather, pad limits, customer delays, and Starship transition attention."}]
attempts.reference_class: Organizations or launch providers that achieved a record annual operational cadence and then needed only low-single-digit growth the next calendar year to cross a round-number threshold, especially where much of the demand is repeatable internal deployment rather than one-off external customers.
attempts.resolution_boundary: True if SpaceX completed 100 or more orbital launches between 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC and 2024-12-31 23:59 UTC; false if it completed 99 or fewer, if launches were suborbital only, or if attempts failed before qualifying as completed orbital launches under the resolution criteria.
attempts.evidence_for: ["SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023, so the threshold requires only 4 additional launches, about 4.2% growth.","Launch count grew from 31 in 2021 to 61 in 2022 to 96 in 2023, showing strong recent cadence growth.","Most launches were Falcon 9 missions supporting Starlink, implying repeatable internal demand and less dependence on external customer readiness.","Operational cadence was already near twice weekly by late 2023, close to the annualized pace needed for 100 launches.","The company had publicly discussed very high launch cadence ambitions, suggesting planning and operational intent above the threshold."]
attempts.evidence_against: ["The threshold still requires sustaining a record cadence for an entire year, not merely demonstrating it briefly in late 2023.","A Falcon 9 grounding could remove enough launch opportunities to miss a narrow 100-launch target.","Range constraints, weather, and pad limits can compound and create schedule slippage.","Customer delays can affect non-Starlink missions and disrupt manifest sequencing.","Transition attention toward Starship could consume organizational focus even if Falcon 9 remains the workhorse."]
attempts.calibration_warnings: ["Base rate is weak because the evidence packet gives only SpaceX's recent launch history, not a broad distribution of organizations sustaining record cadence.","The 2021-2023 growth trend and late-2023 cadence are correlated evidence, so they should not be double-counted as fully independent signals.","The question is close to a threshold: a few launches either way determine resolution, making operational variance unusually important.","No post-2024-01-01 evidence is used, per instruction; actual later outcomes are intentionally ignored.","Public ambition is weak evidence unless backed by concrete capacity, and the packet does not provide detailed pad, booster, or range capacity numbers."]
